step.text: Open functions/index.js with an editor and write the API logic, such as defining a hello route that returns query and body parameters. After finishing, deploy the Functions by running firebase deploy in the terminal. You need to rerun this command after every code modification.

step.name: Step 5: Test API functionality using Postman
step.text: Copy the URL of the deployed Firebase Functions and use Postman to send HTTP requests to test the API. For the hello example, send a GET request with the ?targetID= parameter and a POST request with action and name fields. Confirm that the returned data is correct to verify successful deployment.

step.name: Step 6: Write a complete RESTful API including add, modify, delete, query, and like features
step.text: Use the Express framework in index.js to write multiple routes, including POST / to add articles, PUT /:id to modify articles, DELETE /:id to delete articles, GET / to query article lists, GET /:id to query a single article, and POST /like/:id for the like feature. Each route includes parameter validation and Firestore operations. After completion, deploy with firebase deploy.

step.name: Step 7: Conduct stress testing and verify API stability
step.text: Install the siege tool using brew, then run the command siege -c 100 -r 1 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 'https://us-central1-project.cloudfunctions.net/post/like/id POST {}' to perform a stress test with 100 concurrent tasks. After completion, check Firebase Firestore data for any loss and confirm that the API operates normally under high traffic.

step.name: Step 8: Verify that the API is functioning successfully
step.text: Use Postman or a browser to access the deployed Firebase Functions URL with different HTTP methods and routes. Confirm that add, modify, delete, query, and like functions respond correctly. Also, check Firestore data in the Firebase Console to ensure the entire API service operates without errors.

cb0c68c33994.name: How to Build an AppStore App Review Tracking Notification Slack Bot
cb0c68c33994.description: Use Ruby and Fastlane-SpaceShip to automatically fetch App Store reviews and push notifications to Slack, including the complete process of login authentication, fetching reviews, filtering new reviews, and sending notifications.

step.name: Step 1: Complete App Store Connect Login Authentication Using Fastlane
step.text: Run the command fastlane spaceauth -u <App Store Connect Account (Email)> in the terminal. This command completes the web login including two-factor authentication and saves the login session cookie into the FASTLANE_SESSION file, obtaining the myacinfo token usable for subsequent API requests.

step.name: Step 2: Extend SpaceShip's TunesClient to Retrieve the Latest Review List
step.text: In the Ruby program, extend the SpaceShip::TunesClient class by adding the get_recent_reviews method. Use the API path ra/apps/APP_ID/platforms/ios/reviews?index=page_offset&sort=REVIEW_SORT_ORDER_MOST_RECENT to get up to 100 latest reviews per page and parse the returned JSON.

step.name: Step 3: Use a While Loop to Traverse All Pages and Collect New Reviews
step.text: In the Ruby script, use a while loop starting from index=0 to call get_recent_reviews. Stop the loop when the returned number of reviews is 0. Compare each review’s timestamp with the last execution’s latest time, only collecting reviews newer than the last update time, and update the latest review time to the current newest time.

step.name: Step 4: Assemble Review Messages and Send to Slack
step.text: For the collected new reviews, assemble messages including rating stars, review content, author nickname, version number, region, and time. Use the curl command with the Slack Incoming WebHook URL to send JSON formatted notifications to the specified Slack channel, completing the review push notification.

step.name: Step 5: Verify Successful Receipt of Slack Review Notifications
step.text: After running the Ruby script, confirm the terminal outputs success messages for each review sent, and check the Slack channel for corresponding review notification messages showing the latest App Store review content, indicating success.

d61062833c1a.name: How to Automate Employee Health Status Reporting System with Slack Workflow and Google Sheet
d61062833c1a.description: Automate daily employee health status reporting, generate a list of those who haven't submitted, and send Slack notifications using Slack Workflow Form combined with Google Sheet and App Script, reducing manual comparison and reminder time.

step.name: Step 1: Create a Health Report Google Form and Link it to Google Sheet
step.text: First, create a health report Google Form and check "Collect emails" in the form settings to collect respondents' emails. Then switch to the "Responses" tab at the top of the form, click the Google Sheet icon to link responses to a Google Sheet, and it is recommended to rename the Sheet to "Responses" for easier use later.

step.name: Step 2: Create a Slack Workflow Form Entry Point
step.text: In any Slack chat window, click the blue lightning icon below the input box, select "Open Workflow Builder", click "Create" at the top right to create a new Workflow, name it, choose the trigger as "Shortcut", and set the Channel and display name for this Shortcut. Then add a step, select "Send a form", set the form title and questions, and configure whether fields are required. Save and publish the Workflow after completion.

step.name: Step 3: Connect Slack Workflow Form Responses to Google Sheet
step.text: In Workflow Builder, add a step, select "Add a spreadsheet row" from Google Sheets for Workflow Builder, authorize your Google account, select the previously linked Google Sheet and corresponding Sheet, then set the column names and insert the corresponding form response variables in order, including the respondent's email. Save and publish the Workflow after configuration.

step.name: Step 4: Write Google Sheet App Script to Process Responses and Generate Unfilled List
step.text: In Google Sheet, choose "Tools" -> "Script editor", paste the provided App Script code including formatData() for distributing response data by date, generateUnfilledList() for comparing employee list with daily submissions to generate the unfilled list, and postSlack() for sending reminder messages via Slack Incoming Webhook. Add buttons in the Sheet and assign corresponding functions, then you can manually click the buttons to execute these functions.

step.name: Step 5: Create Slack Incoming Webhook and Set Up Reminder Message Sending
step.text: Install the Incoming WebHooks App in Slack, select the Channel to send reminders to, obtain the Webhook URL, and fill in this URL in the postSlack() function in the App Script. After setting up, run the postSlack() function to send the unfilled list as mentions to the specified Slack Channel.

step.name: Step 6: Import Employee List into Google Sheet
step.text: Create a Slack App, set OAuth scopes including channels:read, users:read, users:read.email, install it to the Workspace and obtain the Bot User OAuth Token. Get the target Channel ID, paste the loadEmployeeList() code into Google Sheet App Script, fill in the Token and Channel ID, then run this function to import member data from the Slack Channel into the employee list Sheet.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Completion of the Automated Health Reporting System
step.text: In the Slack Channel, click the lightning icon, select the newly created Workflow Form, fill out and submit the form, and confirm that the data is successfully written to the Responses and date-grouped Sheets in Google Sheet. Manually run formatData(), generateUnfilledList(), and postSlack() functions to verify that the unfilled list is correctly generated and reminder messages are successfully sent in the Slack Channel.

ba5773a7bfea.name: How to Implement Sharing Functionality Using Visitor Pattern in iOS
ba5773a7bfea.description: This article demonstrates how to implement sharing functionality for multiple models to different platforms in an iOS Swift project using the Visitor Pattern. It includes three data models: UserModel, SongModel, and PlaylistModel, and supports sharing to Facebook, Line, and Instagram.

step.name: Step 1: Define Data Models
step.text: First, define three data structures: UserModel, SongModel, and PlaylistModel, each containing id, name, and related properties. For example, UserModel has profileImageURLString, SongModel has user and coverImageURLString, and PlaylistModel has a songs array and a cover image URL.

step.name: Step 2: Implement Shareable Protocol and Add accept Method
step.text: Make UserModel, SongModel, and PlaylistModel conform to the Shareable protocol and implement the accept(visitor: SharePolicy) method, allowing these models to accept Visitor objects for operations.

step.name: Step 3: Define SharePolicy Protocol and Platform Visitors
step.text: Create the SharePolicy protocol defining the visit(model:) method for UserModel, SongModel, and PlaylistModel respectively. Then implement three classes: ShareToFacebookVisitor, ShareToLineVisitor, and ShareToInstagramVisitor, each implementing the corresponding visit methods to assemble the text and image data required for sharing on each platform.

step.name: Step 4: Use Visitor to Perform Sharing Operation
step.text: Create instances of the Visitor for each platform, such as ShareToInstagramVisitor, and call model.accept(visitor:) to let the model execute the sharing logic through the Visitor, printing the sharing content.

step.name: Step 5: Verify Success
step.text: After running the sharing code, observe the terminal output to check if the shared text and image URLs for each platform are displayed correctly. For example, Instagram sharing prints the corresponding image URL string, and Facebook sharing prints a format including the title and link, confirming that the Visitor Pattern sharing functionality works properly.

d414bdbdb8c9.name: How to Use Google Apps Script to Forward Gmail Emails to Slack
d414bdbdb8c9.description: Use Gmail filters to label emails, then use Google Apps Script to read the labeled email content, customize the format, and automatically forward messages to a specified Slack Channel via Slack Incoming Webhook.

step.name: Step 1: Create a Gmail Filter and Label Emails
step.text: In Gmail, click the advanced search icon at the top right, enter criteria such as sender no_reply@email.apple.com and subject containing is now available to test., click Search to confirm filter results, then click Create filter, set the action to Apply the label, create a new label forward-to-slack. After completion, all emails matching the criteria will be automatically labeled with this tag.

step.name: Step 2: Obtain Slack Incoming Webhook URL
step.text: In Slack, click Apps at the bottom left, search for and add the Incoming WebHooks App, select the Channel to send messages to, after completion the Webhook URL will be displayed. Copy this URL, and you can set the Bot name and avatar. Finally, click Save Settings to save the configuration.

step.name: Step 3: Write and Deploy Google Apps Script Code
step.text: Create a new project in Google Apps Script named ForwardEmailsToSlack, paste the sample code, replace the URL in UrlFetchApp.fetch with the Slack Webhook URL obtained earlier. The script will read emails labeled forward-to-slack, compose a customized message, and send it to Slack.

step.name: Step 4: Manually Run Apps Script and Authorize
step.text: In the Apps Script editor, select the forwardEmailsToSlack function and click Run. The first time you run it, an authorization window will pop up. Click Continue, go to Advanced, select Go to ForwardEmailsToSlack (unsafe), then click Allow to complete authorization. The script will forward labeled emails to Slack.

step.name: Step 5: Set Up Apps Script Trigger for Automatic Scheduling
step.text: In the Apps Script left menu, click Triggers, add a new trigger, select the function forwardEmailsToSlack to run, set the event source to time-driven, choose the execution frequency such as once per hour. After setting, the system will automatically check labeled emails and forward them to Slack on schedule.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Successful Email Forwarding to Slack
step.text: In Gmail, find an email and manually add the forward-to-slack label. After the Apps Script trigger runs, open the specified Slack Channel and confirm if the customized formatted email notification message is received. If the message displays correctly, the forwarding is successful.

e77b80cc6f89.name: How to Integrate Crashlytics with Big Query and Automatically Forward Crash Reports to Slack
e77b80cc6f89.description: This tutorial demonstrates how to configure Crashlytics to import crash data into Big Query, and use Google Apps Script to periodically query the data and automatically forward it to a Slack channel for real-time tracking of app crash status.

step.name: Step 1: Enable Crashlytics Data Export to Big Query
step.text: In your Firebase project, go to the Crashlytics page, click the "•••" menu at the top right, select "Go to BigQuery dataset", follow the official documentation to complete the Big Query service activation and binding, ensuring Crashlytics crash data can be imported into Big Query.

step.name: Step 2: Query Crashlytics Data Using Big Query
step.text: Log in to Google Cloud Platform, navigate to Big Query, select the firebase_crashlytics dataset in the left Explorer, choose the corresponding Table, click "Query" or "COMPOSE NEW QUERY" at the top right to open the SQL editor, enter the official or custom SQL query statement, then click "RUN" to execute the query and view results.

step.name: Step 3: Create Google Apps Script to Connect with Big Query
step.text: Go to the Google Apps Script homepage, log in with the same account as Big Query, create a new project, paste the official example function queryiOSTop10Crashes, and modify the SQL query statement and project ID to complete the integration of Big Query query functionality.

step.name: Step 4: Add Slack Forwarding Functionality
step.text: In the Google Apps Script project, add the sendTop10CrashToSlack function, format the query results into Slack message blocks, and use UrlFetchApp.fetch to send them to the configured Slack Incoming Webhook URL, completing the automatic forwarding feature.

step.name: Step 5: Test and Set Up Scheduled Automatic Execution
step.text: In the Google Apps Script editor, select the sendTop10CrashToSlack function, click Run to execute and complete authentication, confirm the function works properly, then click the clock icon on the left, add a trigger, and set it to run sendTop10CrashToSlack on a schedule to complete the automation.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Success
step.text: Confirm that the Slack channel receives a correctly formatted message of the iOS top crash issues for the past 7 days, and that the query results match the Top 10 crash issues shown on the Crashlytics page, indicating successful integration and automatic forwarding setup.

793cb8f89b72.name: How to Automatically Query and Fill Crashlytics App Crash-Free Users Rate into Google Sheet Using Google Apps Script
793cb8f89b72.description: Query the Crash-Free Users Rate via Google Analytics Data API and use Google Apps Script to automatically fill it into Google Sheet, achieving automated tracking of app crash rate data.

step.name: Step 1: Create Query Conditions Using GA4 Query Explorer
step.text: Open the GA4 Query Explorer tool, sign in with your Google account, and select the corresponding GA4 Property. Set the query date range (for example, using special variables like today, 30daysAgo), add crashFreeUsersRate in metrics, add platform in dimensions, and finally set a dimension filter selecting platform and setting it to iOS or Android. Click Make Request to confirm the query results meet expectations.

step.name: Step 2: Create a Worksheet in Google Sheets and Open the Apps Script Editor
step.text: Create a new worksheet in Google Sheets named App Crash-Free Users Rate. Click Extensions in the top menu, select Apps Script, or directly go to https://script.google.com to create a new project. After entering the Apps Script editor, name the project for easy management.

step.name: Step 3: Add Google Analytics Data API Service and Paste the Query Code
step.text: In the Apps Script editor, click the “+” in Services on the left to add a service, and select Google Analytics Data API. Then convert the Request JSON obtained from GA4 Query Explorer into Apps Script code and paste it into the code block, setting variables such as propertyId, googleSheetID, and googleSheetName.

step.name: Step 4: Run and Authorize the Apps Script Code
step.text: In the Apps Script editor, select the execute function at the top, then click Run or Debug to execute. The first execution will prompt an authorization window; follow the instructions to complete account authorization. After authorization, the script will retrieve the Crash-Free Users Rate from Google Analytics and display the values in the Log.

step.name: Step 5: Automatically Write Crash-Free Users Rate into Google Sheet
step.text: Add code in the execute function to open the specified Google Sheet using SpreadsheetApp.openById and get the specified worksheet. Call the fetchCrashFreeUsersRate function to separately obtain the Crash-Free Users Rate for iOS and Android platforms, combine the date range and data into an array, then use sheet.appendRow to add a new row of data into the worksheet.

step.name: Step 6: Set Up Apps Script Trigger for Scheduled Automatic Execution
step.text: In the Apps Script editor, click the clock icon on the left to enter the trigger settings page, then click the “+ Add Trigger” button at the bottom right. Set the trigger function to execute, choose time-driven as the event source, and set the trigger time to run weekly on schedule. After saving the settings, the system will automatically execute the query and update the Google Sheet according to the schedule.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Crash-Free Users Rate Is Successfully Written into Google Sheet
step.text: Return to Google Sheet and confirm that a new row containing the date range and iOS, Android Crash-Free Users Rate has been added to the worksheet. If the data displays correctly, it indicates the automatic query and fill process has been successfully completed.

a8c2d26cc734.name: How to Implement iOS NSAttributedString HTML Render by Yourself
a8c2d26cc734.description: This article introduces how to use Swift and Foundation's XMLParser to implement HTML string conversion to NSAttributedString rendering by yourself, solving the iOS 15 NSAttributedString DocumentType.html crash and performance issues.

step.name: Step 1: Use XMLParser to Implement HTMLStripper to Remove HTML Tags
step.text: Use Foundation's XMLParser to wrap the HTML string in a custom top-level tag, and implement XMLParserDelegate to extract plain text. Accumulate strings through foundCharacters, and store the accumulated string into the result during didStartElement and didEndElement, completing the removal of HTML tags.

step.name: Step 2: Define HTMLTagParser Protocol and AttributedStringStyle Protocol
step.text: Declare the HTMLTagParser protocol, defining the tag names to parse, storing HTML attributes, and methods to apply styles. Also declare the AttributedStringStyle protocol, defining properties such as font, color, background color, kerning, paragraph style, etc., and implement the render method to apply styles to NSMutableAttributedString.

step.name: Step 3: Implement HTMLStyleAttributedParser to Handle Style Attributes
step.text: Implement the HTMLStyleAttributedParser enumeration supporting CSS style attributes such as color, font-size, line-height, word-spacing, background-color. Provide methods to convert CSS strings into corresponding UIKit properties and apply them to NSMutableAttributedString.

step.name: Step 4: Implement HTMLToAttributedStringParser to Parse HTML and Render Using XMLParser
step.text: Create the HTMLToAttributedStringParser class, implement XMLParserDelegate, and parse the HTML string. Based on the currently parsed tag, apply the corresponding HTMLTagParser and AttributedStringStyle to combine text and styles into NSMutableAttributedString. Support registering multiple tag parsers to extend functionality.

step.name: Step 5: Verify Success
step.text: Test the parsing and rendering functionality using sample HTML strings, including multiple tags and style attributes. Call the parse method of HTMLToAttributedStringParser, confirm that the output NSAttributedString content and styles are correct, and ensure there are no crashes or errors.

48a8526c1300.name: How to Ensure iOS Multilingual String Accuracy Using XCode 13, SwiftGen, and UnitTest
48a8526c1300.description: Ensure Localizable.strings is not accidentally corrupted by using XCode 13's built-in Build Time checks, generating multilingual access objects with SwiftGen, and writing UnitTests to verify the integrity and duplication of multilingual files.

step.name: Step 1: Install the SwiftGen tool
step.text: Add the swiftgen pod to the project's Podfile with the content pod 'SwiftGen', '~> 6.0', then run pod install to complete the SwiftGen installation.

step.name: Step 2: Initialize the SwiftGen configuration file
step.text: Open the terminal, switch to the project directory, run /L10NTests/Pods/SwiftGen/bin/swiftGen config init to generate the swiftgen.yml configuration file, and modify the inputs and outputs fields according to the project’s localization file locations.

step.name: Step 3: Run SwiftGen to generate multilingual access code
step.text: Run the /L10NTests/Pods/SwiftGen/bin/swiftGen command in the terminal to convert Localizable.strings into Swift objects, then drag the generated SwiftGen-L10n.swift file into the project.

step.name: Step 4: Configure XCode Build Phases to run SwiftGen
step.text: In the XCode project settings, select Build Phases, add a new Run Script, paste the script that checks for SwiftGen’s existence and executes it, ensuring the latest multilingual access code is automatically generated on each build.

step.name: Step 5: Write UnitTests to check multilingual file integrity and duplication
step.text: Write UnitTests in the Test Target that read Localizable.strings files for each language from the Bundle, parse Key-Value pairs using regular expressions, check for duplicate Keys within the same file, and compare with the main language file to detect missing or extra Keys.

step.name: Step 6: Verify success
step.text: Build the project, confirm that the SwiftGen script runs correctly during build to generate multilingual access code, and run UnitTests to ensure no errors appear related to duplicate, missing, or extra Keys.

a0c08d579ab1.name: How to Seamlessly Migrate Medium Articles to a Self-Hosted Website
a0c08d579ab1.description: This tutorial explains how to use the Jekyll static site engine and the ZMediumToMarkdown tool to convert Medium articles into Markdown format and deploy them to GitHub Pages. It covers the complete process including environment setup, site configuration, article creation, content synchronization, and custom domain binding.

step.name: Step 1: Install the Ruby Environment
step.text: In the Terminal on macOS Monterey 12.1, enter the command to install Homebrew: /bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)", then install rbenv and ruby-build: brew install rbenv ruby-build. Run rbenv init to initialize, close and reopen the Terminal, finally enter rbenv install 2.6.5 to install Ruby 2.6.5 and use rbenv global 2.6.5 to set the default version.

step.name: Step 2: Install Jekyll, Bundler, and ZMediumToMarkdown
step.text: In the Terminal, enter the command gem install jekyll bundler ZMediumToMarkdown to complete the installation of the Jekyll static site engine, the dependency management tool Bundler, and the Medium to Markdown conversion tool ZMediumToMarkdown.

step.name: Step 3: Create and Configure the Jekyll Blog Project
step.text: Go to https://github.com/cotes2020/chirpy-starter/generate to create a public GitHub repo using the Chirpy template, naming it in the format GithubUsername.github.io. After completion, clone the repo locally using the git clone command in Terminal, then run bundle to install dependencies and execute bundle lock — add-platform x86_64-linux to lock versions. Finally, edit the _config.yml configuration file to replace it with your personal information and site settings.

step.name: Step 4: Download Articles from Medium and Convert to Markdown
step.text: In the local blog directory, use the command ZMediumToMarkdown -j YourMediumUsername to download all articles from Medium and convert them into Markdown format. After completion, preview the articles locally on the site to confirm accuracy.

step.name: Step 5: Push Content to GitHub and Deploy the Website
step.text: In the local blog directory, execute git add ., git commit -m "update post", and git push commands to push the content to the GitHub repo. After pushing, GitHub Actions will automatically run the deployment. Wait about 5 minutes for deployment to complete. After the first deployment, modify the homepage settings in the GitHub repo settings page and redeploy to ensure the website displays correctly.

step.name: Step 6: Bind a Custom Domain
step.text: After purchasing a domain, log into the domain backend and add four Type A Record entries pointing to 185.199.108.153, 185.199.109.153, 185.199.110.153, and 185.199.111.153 respectively. Then return to the GitHub repo Settings, enter the domain name in the Custom domain field, and save. After DNS propagation, you can access the website using the custom domain.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Synchronization and Deployment of Medium Articles
step.text: In the GitHub repo's Actions page, set up the ZMediumToMarkdown automatic sync workflow by configuring the execution schedule and Medium account. Manually or periodically trigger the workflow, confirm that Actions run successfully and trigger site redeployment. Finally, open the self-hosted website in a browser to check if the latest Medium articles display correctly. If so, synchronization and deployment are successful.

f1365e51902c.name: How to Use App Store Connect API to Read and Manage Customer Reviews
f1365e51902c.description: This article explains how to create an App Store Connect API Key, generate a JWT Token, and use the API to read an app's Customer Reviews.

step.name: Step 1: Log in to App Store Connect and Create an API Key
step.text: After logging into the App Store Connect backend, go to 'Users and Access' -> 'Keys' -> 'App Store Connect API', click '+' to add a new API Key, enter a name and select the 'App Manager' permission, then click 'Download API Key' on the right to download and securely save the AuthKey_XXX.p8 file.

step.name: Step 2: Generate a JWT Token
step.text: Use the Ruby sample code to read the previously downloaded AuthKey_XXX.p8 private key file, and set the JWT Header and Payload according to the official documentation, including fields such as kid, iss, iat, exp, and aud. Run the Ruby program to generate the signed JWT Token, which is valid for about 20 minutes.

step.name: Step 3: Use the JWT Token to Call the App Store Connect API
step.text: Use the curl command with the Authorization header including the newly generated JWT Token to call the API endpoint https://api.appstoreconnect.apple.com/v1/apps/APPID/customerReviews, where APPID can be obtained from the App Store Connect backend or the App Store page. A successful response indicates that the app review data has been retrieved.

step.name: Step 4: Verify Successful Retrieval of Customer Reviews
step.text: Confirm that the JSON data returned by the curl command contains the Customer Reviews content and that the data is complete and accurate. This indicates successful reading and management of the app's Customer Reviews using the App Store Connect API. You can then integrate this into automated workflows or backend systems as needed.

e36e48bb9265.name: How to Deploy ZReviewTender App Review Monitoring Bot Using Github Action
e36e48bb9265.description: This tutorial explains how to quickly deploy ZReviewTender via a Github Repo Template to automatically monitor the latest reviews on the App Store and Google Play, and forward the reviews to a Slack Channel.

step.name: Step 1: Go to the ZReviewTender Template Repo and Create a New Private Repo Using the Template
step.text: Open your browser and go to the ZReviewTender-deploy-with-github-action template repository, click the "Use this template" button at the top right, enter your desired repo name, select Private access, and finally click "Create repository from template" to create a new private repository.

step.name: Step 2: Confirm the Newly Created Repo is Private
step.text: Enter the newly created repo page and confirm there is a lock icon and a Private label next to the repo name at the top right. If it is Public, go to Settings -> General -> Danger Zone, click Change repository visibility, and set the repo to Private.

step.name: Step 3: Wait for Project Initialization to Complete and Confirm Actions Status
step.text: Check the badge status on the repo homepage Readme to see if it shows passing, or click the Actions tab above to confirm that the Init ZReviewTender Workflow has run successfully and shows a checkmark, indicating the project initialization is complete.

step.name: Step 4: Confirm Initialization Files and Directories Are Correctly Created
step.text: Return to the Code tab and confirm that the project directory contains the config/ directory, config/android.yml, config/apple.yml files, as well as the latestCheckTimestamp/ directory and .keep file to ensure the initialization files have been created.

step.name: Step 5: Edit and Upload Configuration Files and Keys
step.text: Go to the config/ directory, edit the android.yml and apple.yml configuration files, filling in the corresponding App ID, API key paths, and other settings. After completing, upload the related private key files to the config/ directory and ensure the files have been successfully saved and uploaded.

step.name: Step 6: Manually Trigger the ZReviewTender Workflow
step.text: Click on Actions above, select the ZReviewTender workflow, click the "Run workflow" button on the right to execute it once. After completion, refresh the page, check the execution status and logs to confirm if it ran successfully.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Receipt of Slack Notification Messages
step.text: Confirm whether the specified Slack Channel has received the initialization success message, and check the latest execution status at the top of the repo homepage Readme. If there are no error messages, the deployment is successful. ZReviewTender will automatically crawl and forward the latest reviews every 6 hours.

2724f02f6e7.name: How to Manually Build an HTML Parser and Convert to NSAttributedString
2724f02f6e7.description: This tutorial introduces how to develop an HTML parser from scratch to convert HTML strings into NSAttributedString, covering the complete process including Tokenization, Normalization, Abstract Syntax Tree construction, style conversion, and final rendering.

step.name: Step 1: Use Regular Expressions to Perform HTML Tokenization
step.text: Use regular expressions to match individual tags in the HTML string, including start tags, end tags, and self-closing tags, and save the plain text between tags as rawString. Store the parsing results in an array and use a stack to track start tags, preparing for subsequent tag normalization.

step.name: Step 2: Perform Normalization of HTML Tags
step.text: Traverse the Tokenization results and automatically correct isolated start tags, self-closing tags, and misaligned tags. If a start tag has no corresponding end tag, mark it as isolated and convert it to a self-closing tag; for misaligned tags, insert missing start or end tags to ensure the tag structure is correct.

step.name: Step 3: Convert Normalized Tag Results into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
step.text: Define the Markup protocol and various style node classes, and create the root node RootMarkup. Traverse the Normalization results, create corresponding Markup nodes based on tag types, and form a parent-child tree structure. Plain text nodes are represented as RawStringMarkup to ensure the overall structure is convenient for subsequent operations and extensions.

step.name: Step 4: Convert HTML Tag Attributes into MarkupStyle Styles
step.text: Define the MarkupStyle structure to encapsulate NSAttributedString.Key style attributes, and implement the visitor pattern to convert the style attribute of HTML tags and default styles into MarkupStyle. Traverse the AST and merge styles according to the configured style inheritance strategy to generate the complete style for each node.

step.name: Step 5: Use the Visitor Pattern to Convert the AST into NSAttributedString
step.text: Implement MarkupNSAttributedStringVisitor to recursively traverse AST nodes, collect NSAttributedString from child nodes, and apply the corresponding MarkupStyle to plain text nodes. Finally, combine into a complete NSAttributedString to complete the conversion from HTML string to rich text.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Successful Conversion and Correct Display
step.text: Set the generated NSAttributedString into UITextView or UILabel, and confirm that text styles, links, images, and other elements are correctly displayed. Use unit tests and Snapshot Tests to compare rendering results, ensuring the conversion process is accurate and performance is stable.

e7c547a5be22.name: How to Migrate Medium Articles to a Jekyll Blog and Keep Them Synchronized
e7c547a5be22.description: Use the ZMediumToJekyll tool to automatically download Medium articles, convert them to Markdown, and upload them to a GitHub repository to achieve synchronization with a Jekyll blog.

step.name: Step 1: Create a New GitHub Repository
step.text: Click the green Use this template button, select Create a new repository, enter the repository name, usually your GitHub username or organization name followed by .github.io, choose the public option, then click Create repository from template.

step.name: Step 2: Configure GitHub Actions Permissions
step.text: Go to the newly created repository, click the Settings tab, select Actions -> General, find the Workflow permissions section, choose Read and write permissions, and click Save to save the settings.

step.name: Step 3: Set Medium Username
step.text: Find the _zmediumtomarkdown.yml file in the repository, edit it and enter your Medium username so the tool can automatically download and synchronize your Medium articles.

step.name: Step 4: Manually Run the ZMediumToMarkdown GitHub Action
step.text: Go to the Actions tab of the repository, select the ZMediumToMarkdown workflow, click the Run workflow button, choose the main branch to start execution, and wait for the action to complete downloading and converting articles.

step.name: Step 5: Configure GitHub Pages and Wait for Deployment to Complete
step.text: Go to the repository Settings, select Pages, set the Branch to gh-pages, keep the path as /(root), click Save, and wait for the Pages build and deployment process to complete.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Successful Synchronization of Medium Articles
step.text: After deployment is complete, open your GitHub Pages URL (usually username.github.io) and confirm that the article content and styles display correctly, indicating successful synchronization.

382218e15697.name: How to Create a Github Repo Star Notifier Using Google Apps Script
382218e15697.description: Use Google Apps Script to connect with Github Webhook and forward star notifications to Line, enabling real-time Repo Star notification functionality.

step.name: Step 1: Obtain Line Notify Token and Github Webhook Secret
step.text: Go to the Line Notify website https://notify-bot.line.me/my/, log in and generate an access token. Set the Token Name and notification recipient, then click "Generate token." Copy and save this token. Next, visit https://www.random.org/strings/ to generate a random string of length 32 as the githubWebhookSecret, and record this string for later verification use.

step.name: Step 2: Create and Deploy Google Apps Script Project
step.text: Go to the Google Apps Script website https://script.google.com/home/start, click "+ New Project," and rename the project to My-Github-Repo-Notifier. Paste the provided code into the editor, replacing lineToken and githubWebhookSecret with the values obtained in the previous step. After completion, click "Deploy" in the upper right corner -> "New deployment," select "Web app," set access to "Anyone," click "Deploy," and grant the necessary permissions. After deployment, copy the Web app's Request URL.

step.name: Step 3: Configure Github Webhook and Test Notification
step.text: Log in to Github, go to the target organization or Repo's Settings, click "Webhooks" on the left, then click "Add webhook." In the Payload URL field, enter the Google Apps Script URL with ?secret=githubWebhookSecret appended, for example https://script.google.com/macros/s/XXX/exec?secret=123456. Set Content type to application/json, select "Let me select individual events," uncheck Pushes, check Watches, and click Add webhook. Finally, go back to the Repo and click Star to confirm if a Line Notify push notification is received.

step.name: Step 4: Verify Successful Notification Reception
step.text: After clicking Star on the Github Repo, check if Line Notify receives a notification message containing the stargazer's account, Repo name, and current star count. If the notification is successfully received, it indicates that the Github Webhook and Google Apps Script integration is properly set up and functioning.

5a5c4b25a83d.name: How to Use MITMProxy to Create a Snapshot API Local Mock Server for End-to-End Testing
5a5c4b25a83d.description: This tutorial demonstrates how to use MITMProxy as a reverse proxy server to record and replay API requests and responses, achieving the goal of decoupling API data dependencies during iOS App End-to-End testing.

step.name: Step 1: Modify the App Code to Switch API Host
step.text: Add a condition in the App's Network layer to switch the API Host to the local proxy server address http://127.0.0.1:8080 when the launch argument contains duringE2ETesting; otherwise, use the production remote API. The sample code adds launchArguments to XCUIApplication and switches the api variable accordingly.

step.name: Step 2: Install and Prepare MITMProxy
step.text: Install MITMProxy using Homebrew by running the command brew install mitmproxy. MITMProxy provides tools such as mitmdump, mitmproxy, and mitmweb, with mitmdump used for command-line operations.

step.name: Step 3: Write and Configure the mock.py Script to Implement Record and Replay Functions
step.text: Use the provided mock.py script, which calculates a hash based on the request content and categorizes the response data by path and hash for storage or retrieval. Use config.json to configure ignoring specific Query parameters and Form Data to avoid mapping failures caused by dynamic parameters.

step.name: Step 4: Start MITMProxy Reverse Proxy Server in Recording Mode
step.text: Run the command mitmdump -m reverse:https://yourapihost.com -s mock.py --set record=true --set dumper_folder=addCart --set config_file=config.json to start the reverse proxy and record API Requests and Responses. The data will be stored in the specified directory.

step.name: Step 5: Perform App UI Operations to Complete the Test Flow
step.text: Using the Pinkoi iOS App as an example, manually operate from launching the App, browsing the homepage, selecting products, adding to the cart, and so on. After completion, stop MITMProxy using Ctrl+C and confirm that the recorded API data has been saved.

step.name: Step 6: Start MITMProxy Reverse Proxy Server in Replay Mode and Verify
step.text: Run the command mitmdump -m reverse:https://yourapihost.com -s mock.py --set dumper_folder=addCart --set config_file=config.json to start replay mode. Perform the same UI operations again and confirm that the App can complete the test flow using local response data. Requests outside the recorded scope will return 404.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Use of Snapshot API Local Mock Server for End-to-End Testing
step.text: Confirm that the App can successfully complete the add-to-cart process in replay mode, with all API requests responded to by the local Mock Server. Successful testing indicates that API data dependencies have been decoupled, achieving proof of concept for End-to-End testing.

bd94cc88f9c9.name: How to Build ChatGPT OpenAI API and Slack App Integration
bd94cc88f9c9.description: This tutorial demonstrates how to create a Slack App and use Google Cloud Functions with Python to connect to the OpenAI API, building a ChatGPT Slack Bot with real-time response and interrupt capabilities.

step.name: Step 1: Create Slack App and Configure Permissions
step.text: Go to the Slack App management page https://api.slack.com/apps, click "Create New App" and choose "From scratch", enter the App name and select the Workspace. After completion, go to "OAuth & Permissions" to add Bot permissions chat:write, im:history, im:read, im:write, then click "Install to Workspace" to install the App. Finally, enable the Messages Tab in "App Home" and allow users to send Slash commands and messages from the message tab.

step.name: Step 2: Enable Event Subscriptions and Deploy Google Cloud Functions
step.text: Enable Event Subscriptions in the Slack App, paste the URL of the deployed Google Cloud Functions into the Request URL field and complete verification. Use Python 3.8+ for Google Cloud Functions, write main.py to handle Slack event verification and message reception, and set it to allow anonymous calls. After deployment, add Bot User Event subscription for message.im events in the Slack App and reinstall the App.

step.name: Step 3: Connect OpenAI API to Process Slack Messages and Reply
step.text: Obtain the OpenAI API Key and Slack Bot User OAuth Token, and insert both into the Cloud Functions code. When Slack receives a user message, Cloud Functions will call the OpenAI API to generate a reply and use the Slack API to send the response message. The code implements asynchronous streaming responses and periodically updates the Slack message content to enhance user experience.

step.name: Step 4: Add Message Interrupt Shortcut and Implement Interrupt Function
step.text: In the Slack App management interface under "Interactivity & Shortcuts", add a message Shortcut named "Stop OpenAI API Response" with Callback ID set to abort_openai_api. After reinstalling the App, users can click this Shortcut on messages to mark the message as interrupted. The Cloud Functions code checks if the message metadata is marked as aborted, and if so, interrupts the OpenAI API response stream.

step.name: Step 5: Supplement Thread Context Function to Improve Conversation Coherence
step.text: Add functionality in the Cloud Functions code to retrieve messages from the same Slack thread and classify previous message content by role (user or assistant) to include in the OpenAI API prompt. This allows the Bot to understand context and provide coherent and contextual answers.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Successful Integration of ChatGPT Slack Bot
step.text: Return to the Slack Workspace, send a message to the Slack App, observe if a reply-in-progress indicator appears, then receive the message with the OpenAI API response. Try using the "Stop OpenAI API Response" Shortcut to interrupt the reply and confirm the message stops immediately and shows it has been terminated. If replies are received normally and the interrupt function works, the integration is successful.

f6713ba3fee3.name: How to Use Google Apps Script to Implement Daily Data Report RPA Automation
f6713ba3fee3.description: This tutorial demonstrates how to use Google Apps Script to automatically fetch daily data from Google Analytics and internal system APIs, fill it into Google Sheet reports, set up a schedule for daily automatic execution, and finally build a Web GUI Dashboard to display the data.

step.name: Step 1: Create a Google Apps Script Project and Link to Google Sheet
step.text: In the target Google Sheet, click "Extensions" -> "Apps Script" to create a new Google Apps Script project, or create a project directly from the Google Apps Script homepage. After creation, rename the project and familiarize yourself with the editor features and authorization process.

step.name: Step 2: Write Code to Create or Retrieve the Sheet for the Corresponding Date
step.text: Write the DailyReport class to implement the logic for creating Sheets named by month, including column names, column widths, row heights, frozen rows, and total column settings. Use the SpreadsheetApp API to operate Google Sheets, ensuring that if the current month's Sheet does not exist, it is automatically created.

step.name: Step 3: Connect to Google Analytics API to Fetch Daily Data
step.text: Add the AnalyticsData service in Google Apps Script, use the GA4 Debug Tool to build query conditions, obtain the GA Property ID, write the GAData class to query Sessions data for a specified date, and differentiate device categories (desktop and mobile).

step.name: Step 4: Fill the Retrieved Data into Corresponding Columns in Google Sheet
step.text: Add data filling logic in the DailyReport class, create or retrieve columns based on the date, fill the date, weekday, daily traffic, PC traffic, mobile traffic, and registration numbers into corresponding cells, and set formats and styles.

step.name: Step 5: Set Up a Scheduled Trigger for Daily Automatic Report Updates
step.text: In the Google Apps Script editor, switch to the "Triggers" tab, add a time-driven scheduled trigger, set it to run the cronjob function daily at a specified time (e.g., between 4 AM and 5 AM), to achieve daily automatic report updates.

step.name: Step 6: Build a Web GUI Dashboard to Display Daily Data Reports
step.text: Write the WebDailyReport class to read Google Sheet data and convert it into an HTML table, create HTML templates Web_DailyReport_Scaffolding.html and Web_DailyReport_Body.html, use Ajax to update content periodically, and deploy as a web application for team viewing.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Completion of Automated Report and Dashboard
step.text: Run the debug or cronjob function, confirm that the Google Sheet automatically adds the current or previous day's data column and fills in the correct data, open the deployed Web GUI URL, confirm the data table displays correctly and updates periodically, indicating the automation process is successful.

b04f4fba3cf2.name: How to Backup and Restore iOS Apps and API Data Using mitmproxy and Apple Configurator
b04f4fba3cf2.description: This tutorial explains how to use Apple Configurator 2 to backup and restore .ipa app installation files on an iPhone, and how to use mitmproxy to record and replay the app's API requests and responses, enabling data preservation and replay even after the app is removed from the store.

step.name: Step 1: Install Apple Configurator 2
step.text: Open the Mac App Store, search for and download the Apple Configurator 2 application.

step.name: Step 2: Connect iPhone to Mac and Trust the Computer
step.text: Use a USB cable to connect the iPhone to the Mac. When prompted on the phone to trust this computer, tap Trust and enter the passcode to complete the connection.

step.name: Step 3: Confirm the App to Backup is Installed on the iPhone
step.text: On the iPhone, confirm that the target app is installed to ensure Apple Configurator 2 can extract the app's .ipa file.

step.name: Step 4: Use Apple Configurator 2 to Download and Extract the .ipa File
step.text: In Apple Configurator 2, double-click the iPhone icon to open the info page, switch to the "Apps" tab, click the "+ Add" button in the top right corner, then select "App." Log in with your App Store account, search for the target app and add it. After the download completes, in Finder select "Go" -> "Go to Folder," enter the path ~/Library/Group Containers/K36BKF7T3D.group.com.apple.configurator/Library/Caches/Assets/TemporaryItems/MobileApps, locate the downloaded .ipa file and copy it as a backup. Finally, return to Apple Configurator 2 and click Stop to terminate the operation.

step.name: Step 5: Restore the .ipa File to the iPhone
step.text: Connect the iPhone you want to restore the app to the Mac, open Apple Configurator 2, go to the app adding interface, click "Choose from my Mac…" in the bottom left corner, select the backed-up .ipa file and click "Add." Wait for the installation to complete, then you can reopen the app on the phone.

step.name: Step 6: Install and Configure mitmproxy for API Request Recording
step.text: In the Mac terminal, run brew install mitmproxy to install mitmproxy. Ensure the iPhone and Mac are connected to the same network. Start the mitmproxy service by entering the mitmproxy command. In the iPhone WiFi settings, set the proxy server to the Mac's IP address with port 8080. In the browser, go to http://mitm.it/ to download and install the mitmproxy root certificate. Enable certificate trust settings to complete HTTPS traffic interception.

step.name: Step 7: Use mitmproxy-rodo to Record and Replay API Requests
step.text: Download the mitmproxy-rodo project from GitHub. Edit config.json to exclude parameters that do not affect replay. In the terminal, run mitmdump -s rodo.py --set dumper_folder=zhgchgli --set config_file=config.json --set record=true "~d zhgchg.li" to start recording. Operate the mobile app to generate API requests. After recording, run mitmdump -s rodo.py --set dumper_folder=zhgchgli --set config_file=config.json to replay and verify successful replay.

step.name: Step 8: Verify if Recording and Replay Were Successful
step.text: During replay, confirm that API path pages recorded previously display correctly, while unrecorded pages show network errors. Ensure the replay Hash Hit rate is high, indicating the recorded data is complete and usable.

9903c9783a97.name: How to Self-Host Plane.so Service Using Docker and Configure Nginx Reverse Proxy
9903c9783a97.description: This tutorial explains how to install Plane.so using Docker on macOS, including downloading the installation script, starting the service, backing up and restoring data, and configuring Nginx as a reverse proxy to share port 80 among multiple services accessed via subdomains.

step.name: Step 1: Create Directory and Download Installation Script
step.text: Run mkdir plane-selfhost in the terminal to create a directory, cd into it, then use curl -fsSL -o setup.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/makeplane/plane/master/deploy/selfhost/install.sh to download the installation script. Next, run chmod +x setup.sh to make the script executable.

step.name: Step 2: Verify Docker Environment and Run Installation Script
step.text: Ensure Docker is installed and running locally, then execute ./setup.sh in the plane-selfhost directory. Enter number 1 to start downloading the required Plane Docker image and wait for the image pull to complete.

step.name: Step 3: Edit Plane Environment Configuration File
step.text: Go to the plane-selfhost/plane-app directory and open the .env file with a text editor. Modify settings such as NGINX_PORT (default 80) and WEB_URL (default http://localhost) as needed, then save the file.

step.name: Step 4: Start Plane Service
step.text: Run ./setup.sh again and enter number 2 to start the Plane service. After the service starts, open a browser and go to http://localhost/god-mode/ to set up the initial admin account. The password must include special characters, be longer than 8 characters, and contain uppercase, lowercase letters, and numbers.

step.name: Step 5: Backup Data Using Backup Script
step.text: Create a backup script named plane-backup.sh and run chmod +x plane-backup.sh to make it executable. Use the command ./plane-backup.sh [backup target folder path] [Docker project name] [maximum backup count], for example ./plane-backup.sh /backup/plane plane-app 14. Make sure the Plane service is running.

step.name: Step 6: Restore Data Using Restore Script
step.text: Create a restore script named plane-restore.sh and run chmod +x plane-restore.sh. Execute ./plane-restore.sh, then enter the backup folder path to restore and the Docker project name (leave blank to use the default plane-app). Confirm to proceed with the restore. After completion, restart the Plane service by running ./setup.sh and entering 4.

step.name: Step 7: Configure Nginx Reverse Proxy
step.text: Move the plane-selfhost directory to a unified directory named webServices. Modify NGINX_PORT to 8081 and WEB_URL to http://plane.zhgchg.li in plane-app/.env. In the webServices directory, create a docker-compose.yml file to configure the Nginx service and mount the plane.zhgchg.li.conf configuration file. Create a conf.d directory and the plane.zhgchg.li.conf file, setting proxy_pass to point to the Plane service network name.

step.name: Step 8: Start All Services and Configure DNS
step.text: Create a start.sh script in the webServices directory that first starts the Plane service, then starts the Nginx reverse proxy. Run chmod +x start.sh to make it executable. Execute ./start.sh to launch the services. Finally, add a DNS record for plane.zhgchg.li pointing to the server IP in your internal DNS or local /private/etc/hosts file. After this, you can access Plane via plane.zhgchg.li.

step.name: Step 9: Verify Successful Plane Service Startup
step.text: Open the configured URL (e.g., http://plane.zhgchg.li) in a browser. If you see the Plane homepage and can successfully log in with the admin account, the Docker Plane service and Nginx reverse proxy are set up correctly.

9d0f23784359.name: How to Use Plane.so for Project Management
9d0f23784359.description: This tutorial introduces how to create a Workspace, invite members, create Projects, Issues, Cycles, and use the basic operations of Plane.so, demonstrating a Scrum workflow example.

step.name: Step 1: Create a Workspace
step.text: Upon first entering Plane.so, click to create your first Workspace. A Workspace is a working area, and one account can join multiple Workspaces. It is recommended that small teams use the same Workspace, while large teams can separate teams by Workspace. After creation, you can switch between different Workspaces from the Workspace dropdown menu and enter Workspace Settings for configuration.

step.name: Step 2: Invite Team Members to Join the Workspace
step.text: In Workspace Settings, click Members to invite team members to join the Workspace. Set member roles as Guest/Viewer, Member, or Admin. Guest/Viewer can only view Issues, Comments, and Emojis; Members can operate all functions; Admins can access Settings.

step.name: Step 3: Create and Manage Projects
step.text: Go to the Projects page and click to create a new Project. Set the Project name, description, cover image, and Issue Alias prefix. Set Project permissions to Public or Private and designate the main Project owner. You can add the Project to favorites, publish a public link, and view draft or archived Issues via the menu at the top right.

step.name: Step 4: Create Issues and Set Attributes
step.text: Select the target Project on the left Projects panel and click Create Issue to create an Issue. You can enter a title and description, supporting Markdown, Code Blocks, and drag-and-drop image uploads. Set multiple Assignees, Priority, Modules, Cycle, Labels, Start Date, Due Date, Estimate, and Issue State. Issues can be saved as drafts, and you can use the AI button to input a prompt to automatically generate content.

step.name: Step 5: Create and Manage Cycles
step.text: Manually create Cycles on the Cycle page, setting the name and time period, for example, one Sprint every two weeks. Cycle time periods cannot overlap and cannot be set in the past. An Issue can only be added to one Cycle. Click a Cycle to view details, use different display modes and filter conditions, and view burn-down charts and execution status.

step.name: Step 6: Manage Projects Using the Scrum Workflow
step.text: Following the Scrum process, conduct a Sprint Planning Meeting every Monday to select Issues from the Backlog and add them to the current Cycle, setting Priority and Estimate. Hold Daily Stand-ups to share Issue progress. Change Issue status to To Do or In Progress while working on it, and to Done or Review when completed. Conduct a Sprint Review Meeting every Friday to review completed Issues; unfinished Issues are added to the next Cycle or moved to Pending/Cancel.

step.name: Step 7: Verify Successful Project Management with Plane.so
step.text: Confirm that you have successfully created a Workspace, invited members, created Projects and Issues, and can schedule Issues within Cycles. Check if Issue statuses can be changed according to the Scrum process, and monitor project progress through burn-down charts and execution status. If you can complete these operations, it means you have successfully used Plane.so for project management.

cefdf4d41746.name: How to Join the Medium Partner Program and Start Earning
cefdf4d41746.description: This tutorial provides detailed instructions on how to register a Stripe account, become a Medium Member subscriber, apply for the Medium Partner Program, complete Stripe linking and tax filing, and finally add articles to the Paywall to start earning.

step.name: Step 1: Register a Stripe account
step.text: Go to https://dashboard.stripe.com/register?locale=zh-Hans to register a Stripe account. Follow the on-screen instructions to fill in your personal information and complete the account setup.

step.name: Step 2: Confirm Medium Member subscription
step.text: Visit https://medium.com/manage-membership to confirm that you have subscribed to the Medium Member paid membership. If you have not subscribed yet, please complete the payment to join.

step.name: Step 3: Apply to join the Medium Partner Program
step.text: Go to https://medium.com/me/partner/dashboard, select your country (e.g., Taiwan), confirm that you are a Medium Member and have published at least one article in the past six months, check the agreement box, and click "Enroll now" to apply.

step.name: Step 4: Complete linking between Medium and Stripe
step.text: Follow the on-screen instructions to enter your email and phone number, select the business type as "Individual or sole proprietorship", fill in your personal details, and add bank account information (account holder name, SWIFT/BIC code, account number). Finally, review the information and submit.

step.name: Step 5: Complete tax filing
step.text: On the Medium tax settings page, fill in your personal information in English, select the W-8BEN tax form for non-U.S. citizens, fill in the required details in order, check the agreement box, enter your English name and date, and submit the tax form.

step.name: Step 6: Add articles to the Paywall to start earning
step.text: On the article editing page, click "Manage paywall setting", check "Paywall your story" and save. The article will display a star mark, indicating participation in revenue sharing.

step.name: Step 7: Verify successful enrollment in the Medium Partner Program
step.text: After submitting your application, return to the Payout settings page to confirm that all information shows a checkmark ✅. Then visit https://medium.com/me/partner/dashboard to view your earnings report; if it shows Earnings, your enrollment was successful.

309d0302877b.name: How to Use iOS Shortcuts to Automate SMS Forwarding and Create Reminder To-Dos
309d0302877b.description: Learn how to use iOS Shortcuts to automatically forward specific SMS messages to Line and create reminder to-dos for package deliveries and credit card bills, saving daily management time.

step.name: Step 1: Install and Open the Shortcuts App
step.text: First, install and open the iOS Shortcuts App from the App Store. Make sure you are signed in with your Apple ID and can use automation features.

step.name: Step 2: Create Automation to Automatically Forward SMS
step.text: In the Shortcuts App, switch to the "Automation" tab, tap the "+" icon at the top right to add a new automation, select "Message" as the trigger, set the message to contain keywords (such as http, notification, verification code, authentication code), and change "Ask Before Running" to "Run Immediately."

step.name: Step 3: Add Forward Message Action to Line
step.text: In the automation actions, add a blank action, search for and select Line's "Send Message" action, choose the recipient, change the message content to the "Shortcut Input" "Content," and tap "Done" at the top right after completing the setup.

step.name: Step 4: Create Automation for Package Delivery Reminder
step.text: Similarly, in the Shortcuts App automation page, add a new automation triggered by "Message," set the message to contain "delivered" or "arrived," and set it to "Run Immediately." Add a date adjustment action to add the desired reminder days (e.g., 3 days) to the current date, then add a "Add Reminder" action, set the reminder content to the message content, and set the reminder time (e.g., 2:00 PM). Tap "Done" when finished.

step.name: Step 5: Create Automation for Credit Card Bill Email Reminder
step.text: Make sure the Apple Mail App is installed and signed in, and set mail fetching to push or fetch. In the Shortcuts App automation page, add an automation triggered by "Email," set the subject to include "credit card bill," and change to run immediately. Add a date adjustment action to set the reminder time, use repeat and conditional filtering to match emails with the subject, then add a reminder action to set the reminder content and time. Tap "Done" when finished.

step.name: Step 6: Verify Automation Success
step.text: After completing all automation setups, wait for matching SMS or emails to arrive, confirm whether SMS is automatically forwarded to Line or reminders are automatically added. If it runs normally and triggers even when the phone is locked, the setup is successful.

7584f643c0aa.name: How to Temporarily Fix XCode Build & Run App Black Screen Issue
7584f643c0aa.description: This tutorial explains how to obtain the simulator Device UUID, create a Shell Script to automatically reboot the simulator, and configure XCode Behaviors to avoid black screen issues when running Build & Run multiple times.

step.name: Step 1: Obtain the Simulator Device UUID
step.text: Run the command xcrun simctl list devices in Terminal, find the simulator device name you want to use and its corresponding Device UUID, for example, the UUID for iPhone 15 Pro (iOS 17.5) is 08C43D34–9BF0–42CF-B1B9–1E92838413CC.

step.name: Step 2: Create the auto-reboot.sh Shell Script File
step.text:
In Terminal, navigate to the directory where you want to place the script, run vi auto-reboot.sh, paste the following content and replace [Device UUID] with the UUID you just obtained:
#!/bin/bash
xcrun simctl shutdown [Device UUID]
xcrun simctl boot [Device UUID]
After editing, press the ESC key and type :wq! to save and exit.

step.name: Step 3: Adjust auto-reboot.sh Execution Permissions
step.text: Run chmod +x auto-reboot.sh in Terminal to set the Shell Script you just created as executable, ensuring the script can run properly.

step.name: Step 4: Configure XCode Behaviors to Run the Script
step.text: Open XCode, select Behaviors -> Edit Behaviors, find the Completes option under the Running section (which represents the timing for Stop or Rebuild), check Run, and select the path to the auto-reboot.sh script you created, completing the setup.

step.name: Step 5: Verify if the Black Screen Issue is Successfully Avoided
step.text: Run XCode Build & Run, the simulator will automatically shut down and reboot before each run. Confirm that the simulator no longer gets stuck on a black screen and can start and run the app normally, indicating the setup is successful.

483af5d93297.name: How to Use Github Pages with a Custom Domain
483af5d93297.description: Teach you how to purchase a domain, set up Namecheap DNS, and bind a custom domain to a Github Pages site.

step.name: Step 1: Register and Create Github Pages Repo
step.text: After logging into Github, click the "+" button at the top right and select "New repository", enter the repository name as "yourusername.github.io", set it to public, check "Add a README file", and finally click "Create repository" to create the main Github Pages repo.

step.name: Step 2: Create the Homepage HTML File
step.text: On the newly created repo homepage, click the green "Code" button at the top right, select "+ Create new file", name it index.html, paste your prepared HTML content, then click "Commit changes" at the top right and choose "Commit directly to the main branch" to submit the file.

step.name: Step 3: Purchase a Domain and Log into Namecheap
step.text: Go to the Namecheap homepage, use the search function to find a domain name you like, after confirming the domain is available, click "Add To Cart" to add it to your cart, then click "Checkout", fill in your credit card information, and complete the payment to purchase the domain.

step.name: Step 4: Set Namecheap DNS Servers and A Records
step.text: After logging into your Namecheap account, go to Account -> Dashboard, find the domain you just purchased and click "Manage", confirm the domain's NAMESERVERS are set to "Namecheap BasicDNS", switch to the "Advanced DNS" tab, click "ADD NEW RECORD", add four A Records with Host as "@" and Values as 185.199.108.153, 185.199.109.153, 185.199.110.153, 185.199.111.153 respectively, saving each record after input.

step.name: Step 5: Add a CNAME Record Pointing to Github Pages
step.text: In Namecheap's "Advanced DNS" tab, click "ADD NEW RECORD", select Type as CNAME Record, enter the subdomain you want to set (e.g., www) as Host, enter your Github Pages URL "yourusername.github.io" as Value, then save the settings.

step.name: Step 6: Configure Custom Domain in Github Repo
step.text: Return to the Github repo, click Settings -> Pages, enter your domain name (www can be omitted) in the Custom domain field, click Save, check the box to enforce HTTPS, and wait for DNS verification to complete.

step.name: Step 7: Verify if the Custom Domain is Successfully Activated
step.text: Wait for the DNS settings to take effect (usually within five minutes, up to 72 hours), then open your browser and enter your custom domain URL (e.g., https://yourdomain). If your Github Pages site content displays correctly, the custom domain setup is successful.

0095528cf875.name: How to Deploy ZReviewTender App Store Review Monitoring Bot Using Github Action
0095528cf875.description: Set up Slack App, generate Apple and Google Play API credentials through three main steps, deploy Github Action to monitor App Store reviews for free, and finally verify if the setup is successful.

step.name: Step 1: Create and Configure Slack App
step.text: Go to https://api.slack.com/apps, click "Create New App" at the top right, choose From scratch, enter an App name such as ZReviewTender, select the target Workspace and create it. Go to the OAuth & Permissions tab, add three Scopes: chat:write, chat:write.public, and links:write. Return to the Install App tab, click "Install to YOUR_WORKSPACE" and allow permissions. Copy the Slack App Bot User OAuth Token, create a Slack channel to receive notifications, and if it is a private channel, add the Slack App to the channel. Finally, obtain the Channel ID of that channel for later configuration.

step.name: Step 2: Generate Apple App Store Connect API and Google Play Developer API Credentials
step.text: Obtain the Issuer ID on the Keys page of App Store Connect, create a new API Key and download the AuthKey_XXXXXXXXXX.p8 file, note the Private Key ID and App ID. Enable the Android Developer API in Google Cloud Console, create a Service Account and download the JSON key file, note the Service Account Email. Add this Email to Users and permissions in Google Play Console and grant App permissions. Note the Package Name, Developer ID, and App ID in Google Play Console for later configuration.

step.name: Step 3: Deploy Github Action and Fill in Configuration Files
step.text: Go to https://github.com/ZhgChgLi/ZReviewTender-deploy-with-github-action, create a Private Repo, and enable Github Actions in Repo Settings. Run the Init ZReviewTender workflow to initialize the project. Upload the Apple .p8 file and Google JSON key downloaded in Step 2 to the /config directory. Edit config/apple.yml to enter Apple API information as well as Slack OAuth Token and Channel ID. Edit config/android.yml to enter Google Play API information as well as Slack OAuth Token and Channel ID. Commit the changes to the main branch.

step.name: Step 4: Verify if the Setup is Successful
step.text: In the Github Repo's Actions tab, select the ZReviewTender workflow and click Run workflow to manually execute it once. After the execution completes, check if the Slack channel receives the startup message. If the message is received, the deployment is successful; if errors occur or no message is received, please check if the configuration files and Github Actions permission settings are correct.

6922e90ba90c.name: How to Quickly Migrate Line Notify to Telegram Bot Notifications
6922e90ba90c.description: Step-by-step guide on how to migrate Line Notify personal notification service to the free and more powerful Telegram Bot, including applying for a Telegram Bot, creating a notification group, obtaining the group Chat ID, sending messages, and using Google Apps Script to implement notification sending and command interaction.

step.name: Step 1: Apply for a Telegram Bot
step.text: After installing and registering for Telegram, open Telegram, search for and add the official BotFather bot as a friend, send the message "/newbot" to create your bot, enter the bot name and username in sequence (the username must end with bot and be unique), then obtain your BOT API Token. Please keep this Token safe.

step.name: Step 2: Create a Telegram Notification Group and Add the Bot
step.text: Create a new group in Telegram, add the bot account you just applied for to this group, and set the group name and avatar to easily distinguish different types of notification groups.

step.name: Step 3: Obtain the Telegram Group Chat ID
step.text: Use the curl command to call the Telegram API with the format https://api.telegram.org/botYour_BOT_API_Token/getUpdates to get the list of bot messages. Find the chat.id in the response where the group name matches and the type is group. This value is the group Chat ID. If the response is empty, send a message in the group and call the API again.

step.name: Step 4: Use Telegram API to Send Messages to the Group
step.text: Use the curl command to call the Telegram API sendMessage endpoint with the format https://api.telegram.org/botYour_BOT_API_Token/sendMessage, including the chat_id (group Chat ID) and text (message content) parameters. Upon success, the message will appear in the group.

step.name: Step 5: Use Google Apps Script to Migrate Line Notify Messages to Telegram Bot
step.text: Write a new function in Google Apps Script using the Telegram Bot API Token and group Chat ID. Send notification messages by making POST requests to Telegram's sendMessage endpoint, and optionally set parse_mode to Markdown to enrich message formatting. Replace the original Line Notify sending code with this.

step.name: Step 6: Implement Telegram Bot Commands and Deploy Webhook
step.text: Create a new project in Google Apps Script and write a doPost function to handle commands received by the Telegram Bot, such as /update, and respond with specified messages. After completing the code, deploy it as a web app via Google Apps Script, set access to anyone, and obtain the Webhook URL.

step.name: Step 7: Register Telegram Bot Webhook and Verify Functionality
step.text: Use curl to call the Telegram API setWebhook endpoint and register the Google Apps Script Webhook URL to the Telegram Bot. After completion, test by sending commands to the bot or group in Telegram to confirm the Bot responds correctly and sends notifications, indicating a successful migration.

1e85b8df2348.name: How to Build a Free GA4 Automated Data Notification Bot
1e85b8df2348.description: Use Google Apps Script to connect Google Analytics 4 with Telegram Bot to automatically send website data notifications.

step.name: Step 1: Generate Data Report Query Parameters Using GA4 Query Explorer
step.text: Go to the official GA4 Query Explorer tool, select your property ID, set the query date range (e.g., 7daysAgo to yesterday), choose the metrics and dimensions you want to query, set the data aggregation method to total, and set the limit to 10. After clicking MAKE REQUEST, note the generated JSON query parameters, which will be used later when writing the Google Apps Script.

step.name: Step 2: Create Google Apps Script and Query Data Using Google Analytics Data API
step.text: Go to https://script.google.com/home to create a new project, name the project, click Services and add the Google Analytics Data API. Convert the previously generated query parameters into Google Apps Script code, write the fetchScreenPageViews function to call AnalyticsData.Properties.runReport, and authorize when running the code to ensure the account has access to the corresponding GA report.

step.name: Step 3: Combine Google Apps Script with Telegram Bot to Send Data Notifications
step.text: Create a Telegram Bot as described in previous articles, obtain the Bot Token and Channel Chat ID. In Google Apps Script, write the execute function to call fetchScreenPageViews to get data, compose the message string, and use the sendNotifyMessage function to send the message to the specified channel via the Telegram Bot API. Run the execute function to confirm success.

step.name: Step 4: Set Up Scheduled Automatic Execution
step.text: In the Google Apps Script editor, click Triggers in the left menu and add a new trigger. Choose the execute function to run, select deployment as Head, set the event source to time-driven, configure the trigger frequency and time period, and set failure notification frequency before saving. After completion, the system will automatically run the script according to the schedule and regularly send GA4 data notifications.

step.name: Step 5: Verify Success
step.text: After running the execute function, check the Logger output in Google Apps Script to see if GA4 data was correctly retrieved, and confirm that the Telegram channel received the expected data notification message. If the message is successfully delivered and the data is accurate, the automated data notification bot has been successfully created.

70aeddb1fd9b.name: How to Quickly Create a Free Personal LinkTree-like Link Page Using GitHub Pages
70aeddb1fd9b.description: Quickly create a customized and free personal link page by forking the linkyee template repository and setting up GitHub Pages, supporting custom domains and dynamic variables.

step.name: Step 1: Fork the linkyee template repository and create a new repository
step.text: Go to the linkyee template repository page, click the "Use this template" button at the top right, select "Create a new repository", check "Include all branches", enter the desired GitHub Pages repository name, and finally click "Create repository" to complete the fork.

step.name: Step 2: Adjust GitHub Actions permission settings
step.text: Enter the newly forked repository, click Settings -> Actions -> General, set Actions permissions to "Allow all actions and reusable workflows", set Workflow permissions to "Read and write permissions", and click Save to save the changes.

step.name: Step 3: Configure the GitHub Pages branch
step.text: In the repository's Settings -> Pages page, set the GitHub Pages branch to "gh-pages", and confirm the page shows the message "Your site is live at: XXXX", indicating the site has been successfully deployed.

step.name: Step 4: Wait for GitHub Actions to complete the initial deployment
step.text: Go to Settings -> Actions, monitor the automatic deployment process, wait for the first deployment to complete, and ensure no errors occur.

step.name: Step 5: Verify if the GitHub Pages URL is accessible
step.text: Open the GitHub Pages URL in a browser (e.g., https://your-username.github.io/Repo-Name), confirm the page correctly displays the personal link page, indicating a successful deployment.

4cb4437818f2.name: How to Use Google Apps Script Web App Form to Integrate with Github Action CI/CD Workflow
4cb4437818f2.description: Create a Web App form with Google Apps Script, connect to the Github API to get the branch list, and trigger Github Action to execute CI/CD workflows. Suitable for non-engineering users within an organization.

step.name: Step 1: Create a Google Apps Script Project and Add a Form File
step.text: Go to the Google Apps Script website and create a new project. Click the '+' in the file list on the left to add a new file named Form.html. Paste the ChatGPT-generated HTML form template content, including email field, version number, branch selection, and update content fields.

step.name: Step 2: Write doGet and processForm Functions and Deploy the Web App
step.text: In Code.gs, write the doGet function to load Form.html and pass user email, title, version number, and an empty branch list. Write the processForm function to handle form submission data. Then click 'Deploy' -> 'New deployment' -> 'Web app' at the top right, set execution identity to 'User accessing the web app', and access permission to 'Users in the same organization'. Complete deployment and obtain the Web App URL.

step.name: Step 3: Connect to Github API to Get PR Branch List and Update doGet
step.text: Add a Github.gs file and write the githubAPI function to access Github using the RESTful API. In doGet, call githubAPI to get pull requests of the specified repo and pass the returned branch list into the HTML template so the form's branch options display dynamically.

step.name: Step 4: Call Github API in processForm to Trigger Github Action Workflow
step.text: In processForm, validate that the user has filled in the version number and branch. Construct a payload object including ref and inputs. Use the githubAPI function to call the Github Action workflow dispatch API with POST method to trigger the specified workflow. Return success or error messages to the frontend for display.

step.name: Step 5: Redeploy the Web App and Verify Form Functionality
step.text: After each GAS code modification, create a new version in deployment management and redeploy. Then open the form using the Web App URL, confirm that the form displays the user email and branch list correctly, and that submitting the form successfully triggers Github Action. Receiving a success message indicates integration is complete.

fd719053b376.name: How to Use XCFolder to Convert XCode Virtual Directories into Physical Directories
